title: Dog Skipping with Rear Leg: Causes and When to See a Vet
meta_desc: Is your dog hopping or skipping on a back leg? Learn about luxating patellas, urgency levels, and how to help your pet today.
content_html:
Understanding the Skipping Gait

If you notice your dog occasionally lifting a hind leg for a few steps before returning to a normal walk, they are displaying what vets often call a skipping gait. This is most commonly caused by a luxating patella, a condition where the kneecap temporarily slips out of its natural groove. While it may look like a minor quirk, it is often a sign of joint instability.

Is This an Emergency?

Urgency Level: Medium. In most cases, a skipping leg is not a life-threatening emergency, but it does require professional evaluation. You should schedule a veterinary exam within the next 48 to 72 hours. However, if your dog is vocalizing in pain, refusing to put any weight on the leg at all, or if the limb appears swollen, you should contact an emergency clinic immediately.

Why a Photo or Video Helps Triage

Because the skipping often happens intermittently, your dog may walk perfectly fine once they are inside the exam room. This makes it difficult for a veterinarian to see the issue in real-time. Capturing a clear video of your dog walking and skipping, or a photo of how they hold their leg at rest, provides vital diagnostic evidence that helps determine the severity of the issue and the necessary treatment plan.

faqs.question: What breeds are most prone to skipping on a back leg?
faqs.answer: Small and toy breeds, such as Chihuahuas, Pomeranians, and Yorkies, are genetically predisposed to luxating patellas, though it can occur in larger breeds as well.

faqs.question: Will my dog need surgery for a skipping leg?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily. Depending on the grade of the luxation, your vet may recommend weight management, physical therapy, or joint supplements before considering surgical options.

faqs.question: Can I give my dog human pain medication for the skip?
faqs.answer: No. Never give your pet human medications like ibuprofen or acetaminophen, as these can be highly toxic to dogs. Always consult your vet for pet-safe anti-inflammatories.
